title: False Teachers and Their Destruction
subtitle: Past judgments as paradigm, present deception exposed, future doom certain

heb.paragraph: False teachers (pseudodidaskaloi) will arise - as false prophets arose in Israel, so false teachers will infiltrate the church.
Destructive heresies (haireseis apoleias) - teachings that lead to destruction. The word means a chosen opinion or faction.
Many will follow their sensuality - false teaching produces moral decay. Doctrine and ethics are inseparable.

refs.note: Beware of false prophets who come in sheep's clothing.
Some will depart from the faith, devoting themselves to deceitful spirits.
Certain people have crept in unnoticed, perverting grace into sensuality.
Fierce wolves will come in, not sparing the flock.

notes.note: False teachers will secretly bring destructive heresies, even denying the Master who bought them - bringing swift destruction on themselves.
Many will follow their sensuality. In greed they will exploit you. Their condemnation is not idle; their destruction is not asleep.

Calvin emphasizes that false teachers deny the Master who bought them - language of apostasy. They exploit for greed. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Master who bought them (ton agorasanta) uses marketplace language. Christ purchased them; they reject the transaction. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

Their condemnation from long ago is not idle - the verdict was rendered in eternity; execution awaits. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes that the false teachers combine doctrinal error (denying the Master) with moral license (sensuality). Heresy and sin go together. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids observes that the parallel with Jude 4 is striking. Both epistles address the same crisis of false teaching. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.historical: Second Peter addresses the threat of false teachers who deny Christ's return (parousia) and promote moral license. The author presents himself as the apostle Peter writing shortly before his death (1:14), though many scholars date the letter later due to its developed theology, reference to Paul's letters as 'Scripture' (3:16), and literary dependence on Jude. The false teachers mock the delay of Christ's return ('Where is the promise of his coming?' 3:4) and use this skepticism to justify immoral living. The letter defends apostolic eyewitness testimony (1:16-18), warns of judgment using Old Testament examples (2:4-10), and explains that God's patience allows time for repentance (3:9).
hist.context: Peter warns that as there were false prophets in Israel, so there will be false teachers among them. They will secretly bring in destructive heresies, even denying the Master who bought them. Many will follow their sensuality, bringing Christianity into disrepute. In greed they will exploit with fabricated words. Their condemnation has not been idle; their destruction has not been asleep.

heb.paragraph: Cast them into Tartarus (tartarosas) - the only NT use of this word. Tartarus was the deepest abyss in Greek mythology; Peter uses it for angelic imprisonment.
The ancient world - the pre-flood generation. God did not spare the ancient world but preserved Noah.
An example (deigma) of what is going to happen - Sodom and Gomorrah illustrate the fate of the ungodly.

refs.note: The sons of God saw the daughters of man - angelic sin.
All flesh died that moved on the earth.
The LORD rained sulfur and fire on Sodom and Gomorrah.
Angels who did not stay in their position; Sodom and Gomorrah.

notes.note: God did not spare angels who sinned but cast them into Tartarus, committed to chains of gloomy darkness until judgment.
God did not spare the ancient world but preserved Noah, a herald of righteousness, with seven others, when he brought the flood.
God condemned Sodom and Gomorrah, making them an example. He rescued Lot, who was tormented by the lawless conduct he saw and heard.
The Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trials and to keep the unrighteous under punishment for the day of judgment.

Calvin notes the double lesson: judgment on the wicked (angels, flood, Sodom) and rescue of the righteous (Noah, Lot). Both are certain. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Tartarus is not Hades but a special prison for angels. Peter uses Greek terminology to describe an OT/Jewish concept.
Noah was a herald (keruka) of righteousness - he preached by word and example. He warned of coming judgment.
Righteous Lot (dikaion Lot) is surprising given Genesis. Peter follows Jewish tradition that exalted Lot's righteousness.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes that the three examples prove God's faithfulness in judgment and rescue. The false teachers will not escape. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

hist.context: Peter gives three examples of divine judgment. First, God did not spare angels who sinned but cast them into Tartarus, committed to chains of gloomy darkness. Second, God did not spare the ancient world but preserved Noah, a herald of righteousness, when he brought flood on the ungodly. Third, God condemned Sodom and Gomorrah to extinction, making them an example for the ungodly. But God rescued righteous Lot, distressed by the sensuality of the lawless. The Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trials and keep the unrighteous under punishment.

heb.paragraph: Bold and willful (tolmetai authadeis) - the false teachers are brash and self-willed, not intimidated by angelic powers.
Irrational animals (aloga zoa) - the false teachers are compared to beasts, driven by instinct, born for capture and destruction.
The dog returns to its own vomit - a proverb illustrating the revolting return to sin. Apostasy is disgusting.

refs.note: They rush for profit into Balaam's error.
The last state is worse than the first.
Like a dog that returns to his vomit.

notes.note: Bold and willful, they blaspheme glorious ones. Like irrational animals, they are creatures of instinct, born for destruction.
They revel in dissipation, feasting with you. Eyes full of adultery, enticing unstable souls. They follow Balaam's way.
Waterless springs, mists driven by storms. They speak bombast, entice through sensuality. Promising freedom, they are slaves of corruption.
Those who escape defilement but become entangled again are worse off. The dog returns to vomit; the sow to mud.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Like irrational animals - the comparison is to wild beasts caught in traps. They are destined for destruction.
Balaam loved the wages of unrighteousness - greed motivated his willingness to curse Israel.
The dog proverb is from Proverbs 26:11. The sow proverb may be from extrabiblical tradition.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner sees the portrait as graphic and repulsive. The imagery - dogs, pigs, vomit, mud - evokes visceral disgust at apostasy. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes extensive parallels with Jude. Both letters draw on common tradition to describe false teachers. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Peter describes the false teachers in vivid terms. They are bold and willful, blaspheming glorious ones where angels do not. They are like irrational animals, born to be caught and destroyed. They revel in daytime dissipation, feasting with believers while indulging in deception. Their eyes are full of adultery; they entice unstable souls; their hearts are trained in greed. They follow Balaam's way, who loved gain. They are waterless springs, mists driven by storms, destined for blackness. They speak bombast but entice through sensuality. Promising freedom, they are slaves of corruption. Those who escape defilement through knowledge of Christ but become entangled again are worse off than before. Better not to have known than to turn back. The proverb applies: the dog returns to vomit; the sow returns to mud.

lit.note: Chapter 2 exposes false teachers: their origin, their doom (proven by OT examples), and their repulsive character.

themes.note: Judgment dominates: God judged angels, the flood generation, and Sodom. Justice appears in certain punishment for false teachers.

coaching.tip: 'There will be false teachers among you, who will secretly bring in destructive heresies' (v. 1). The future becomes present: false teachers aren't coming; they're here. Their method is stealth ('secretly'), their product is heresy, their motive is greed (v. 3). Peter names the threat so the church can recognize it. Diagnosis precedes cure.

Three examples of judgment (vv. 4-9): angels who sinned, Noah's world, Sodom and Gomorrah. Three examples of rescue: Noah preserved, Lot delivered, the righteous protected. Peter's point: God knows how to judge the unrighteous while rescuing the godly. Past patterns predict future outcomes. History teaches theology.

prayer_prompt: As Peter exposes false teachers who exploit believers with fabricated stories, consider asking God to sharpen your discernment — to recognise teaching that tells you what you want to hear rather than what is true.
